perturbation.original_text: During the term of this Agreement, all actions taken by the Contractor pursuant to this Agreement shall be in the capacity of an independent contractor, and in no event shall the Contractor have any obligations under the Notes.
perturbation.changed_text: During the term of this Agreement, all actions taken by the Contractor pursuant to this Agreement shall be in the capacity of an independent contractor, however, the contractor is responsible for all debts and obligations of the company, including those under the Notes.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the contractor is an independent contractor but is responsible for the debts and obligations of the company, we are introducing a contradiction. Under common law, an independent contractor is not typically responsible for the debts of the entity they are contracted with.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law - Independent Contractor vs. Employee Liability
perturbation.law_citation: Varies by state, but generally aligns with the Restatement (Second) of Agency § 220
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text states that the contractor is responsible for all debts and obligations of the company including the 'Notes'. This directly conflicts with the established principle that independent contractors are generally not liable for the debts of the companies they work for. This misclassification undermines the contractor's status as an independent entity and introduces uncertainty in their financial obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: independent contractor | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you independent contractor Distinguished from employees , independent contractors are entities hired by employers to accomplish certain tasks but do not have the right of control over those entities. Independent contractors tend to have specialized skills or knowledge and work for many employers. Under tort law , employers who hire independent contractors usually have no vicarious liabilities to independent contractors’ tortious acts. While the duties of certain conducts are non-delegable, employers will remain vicariously liable . These conducts include: (a) inherently dangerous activities , (b) duties arising out of a relationship with a specific plaintiff or the public, (c) duties to keep premises opened to the public in a reasonably safe condition of storekeepers or landowners to invitees, and (d) duties to comply with state safety statutes in a minority of jurisdictions. In addition, employers may be liable for their own negligence in selecting independent contractors if they were negligent in hiring them. Employers are also liable for any physical harm caused by any act committed by independent contractors pursuant to orders or directions negligently given by employers. Under tax law , independent contractors must pay their own Social Security and Medicare taxes, while employers generally pay for employees. The U.S. Department of Labor has an employee/independent contractor comparison chart here . [Last reviewed in March of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex CIVICS taxation COMMERCE LIFE EVENTS employment employee benefits labor law business law business organizations wex definitions employment law group rights individual rights legal education and practice tax Wex Toolbox
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Independent contractor (self-employed) or employee? | Internal Revenue Service Skip to main content Independent contractor (self-employed) or employee? English Español 中文 (简体) 中文 (繁體) 한국어 Русский Tiếng Việt Kreyòl ayisyen More In File Individuals Businesses and self-employed Business tax account Employer ID numbers Business taxes Business structures Operating a business Self-employed Small business Industries and professions Small business events Online learning Large business Charities and nonprofits International taxpayers Governmental liaisons Federal, state and local governments Indian tribal governments Tax exempt bonds Taxpayer identification numbers (TIN) It is critical that business owners correctly determine whether the individuals providing services are employees or independent contractors. Generally, you must withhold and deposit income taxes, Social Security taxes and Medicare taxes from the wages paid to an employee. Additionally, you must also pay the matching employer portion of Social Security and Medicare taxes as well as pay unemployment tax on wages paid to an employee. Generally, you do not have to withhold or pay any taxes on payments to independent contractors. Select the scenario that applies to you: I am an independent contractor or in business for myself. If you are a business owner or contractor who provides services to other businesses, then you are generally considered self-employed. For more information on your tax obligations if you are self-employed (an independent contractor), see our Self-employed individuals tax center . I hire or contract with individuals to provide services to my business. If you are a business owner hiring or contracting with other individuals to provide services, then you must determine whether the individuals providing services are employees or independent contractors. Follow the rest of this page to find out more about this topic and what your responsibilities are. Determining whether the individuals providing services are employees or independent contractors Before you can determine how to treat payments you make for services, you must first know the business relationship that exists between you and the person performing the services. The person performing the services may be: An independent contractor An employee (common-law employee) A statutory employee A statutory nonemployee A government worker In determining whether the person providing service is an employee or an independent contractor, all information that provides evidence of the degree of control and independence must be considered. Common law rules Facts that provide evidence of the degree of control and independence fall into three categories: Behavioral : Does the company control or have the right to control what the worker does and how the worker does his or her job? Financial : Are the business aspects of the worker’s job controlled by the payer? (these include things like how worker is paid, whether expenses are reimbursed, who provides tools/supplies, etc.) Type of relationship : Are there written contracts or employee type benefits (that is, pension plan, insurance, vacation pay, etc.)? Will the relationship continue and is the work performed a key aspect of the business? Businesses must weigh all these factors when determining whether a worker is an employee or independent contractor. Some factors may indicate that the worker is an employee, while other factors indicate that the worker is an independent contractor. There is no “magic” or set number of factors that “makes” the worker an employee or an independent contractor and no one factor stands alone in making this determination. Also, factors which are relevant in one situation may not be relevant in another. The keys are to look at the entire relationship and consider the extent of the right to direct and control the worker. Finally, document each of the factors used in coming up with the determination. Remote worker An individual working remote
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the principle that independent contractors are not responsible for the debts of the companies they work for. The original text clearly states the contractor has no obligations under the Notes. The changed text imposes responsibility for all company debts, including those under the Notes, while still classifying the contractor as an independent contractor. This is a misalignment of terminology, as 'independent contractors are entities hired by employers to accomplish certain tasks but do not have the right of control over those entities,' as noted in Scraped Law Snippet 1. By making the contractor responsible for the company's debts, it implies a level of financial control or responsibility not typically associated with an independent contractor, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Contractor shall establish and maintain at all times during the term of this Agreement files and records (including, without limitation, computerized records) regarding the Notes and the Note Portfolio, with full and correct entries of all transactions or modifications in a reasonably secure, up-to-date manner and in accordance with the following:
perturbation.changed_text: The Contractor shall establish and maintain at all times during the term of this Agreement files and records (including, without limitation, computerized records) regarding the Notes and the Note Portfolio, with incomplete entries of all transactions or modifications in a reasonably insecure manner and in accordance with the following:
perturbation.explanation: This modification mandates the maintenance of records with incomplete and incorrect entries, and in an insecure manner. This contradicts basic legal principles of financial record-keeping.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Sarbanes-Oxley Act requires publicly traded companies and their contractors to maintain accurate and secure financial records. The modified text directly contradicts this requirement by mandating 'incomplete entries' and 'insecure manner' of record-keeping. This would violate internal control reporting standards and potentially expose the Company and Contractor to legal liability.

Management assessment of internal controls (a) Rules required The Commission shall prescribe rules requiring each annual report required by section 78m(a) or 78o(d) of this title to contain an internal control report, which shall- (1) state the responsibility of management for establishing and maintaining an adequate internal control stru
perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates maintaining records with 'incomplete entries' and in an 'insecure manner.' This directly contradicts the principle of maintaining adequate internal controls. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 7262, explicitly tasks the Commission with prescribing rules requiring annual reports to contain an internal control report, which includes 'establishing and maintaining an adequate internal control structure.' The changed text, by requiring incomplete and insecure records, actively undermines the establishment and maintenance of an adequate internal control structure, creating a direct legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any dispute shall be heard in the courts of the state of Minnesota.
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute shall be heard in the private arbitration forum pre-selected by the Contractor, regardless of the nature of the dispute or the location of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: This modification forces all disputes into a private arbitration forum selected solely by the Contractor, potentially overriding mandatory state or federal jurisdictional requirements and the rights of other parties to access courts.
perturbation.law_citation: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Federal Arbitration Act generally supports arbitration agreements, this modification attempts to enforce an arbitration clause that could be deemed unconscionable due to the exclusive control the Contractor has over the selection of the forum. This could be viewed as violating the principle of fairness and neutrality required in arbitration proceedings, rendering the clause unenforceable under the FAA or applicable state law.

Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising ou
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the principle of fairness and neutrality required in arbitration proceedings. By allowing the Contractor to unilaterally select the arbitration forum, it creates a situation where the impartiality of the process is questionable, potentially violating the spirit of the FAA and other state laws governing arbitration, as the Law Explanation states. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 9 USC 2, generally enforces agreements to arbitrate. The changed text attempts to enforce an arbitration agreement that could be deemed unconscionable due to the exclusive control the Contractor has over the selection of the forum.
